text: Seeing she bore no children, Rachel envied her sister and told Jacob to give her children or she would die.
Jacob grew angry, asking if he were in God's place, who had withheld children from her.
Rachel offered her maid Bilhah to Jacob, so she could have children through her.
She gave him Bilhah as a wife, and Jacob slept with her.
Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son.
Rachel named him Dan, saying God had judged her and given her a son.
Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.
Rachel named him Naphtali, saying she had wrestled with her sister and prevailed.
Seeing she had stopped bearing, Leah gave her maid Zilpah to Jacob as a wife.
Zilpah bore Jacob a son.
Leah named him Gad, saying good fortune had come.
Zilpah bore Jacob a second son.
Leah named him Asher, saying women would call her happy.
During wheat harvest Reuben found mandrakes and brought them to his mother Leah; Rachel asked for some.
Leah objected that Rachel had taken her husband too; Rachel offered Jacob's night in exchange for the mandrakes.
That evening Leah met Jacob and told him he must sleep with her, since she had hired him with the mandrakes.
God listened to Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.
She named him Issachar, saying God had rewarded her for giving her maid to her husband.
Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son.
She named him Zebulun, saying now her husband would honor her, since she had borne him six sons.
Afterward she bore a daughter and named her Dinah.
Then God remembered Rachel, listened to her, and opened her womb.
She conceived and bore a son, saying God had taken away her disgrace.
She named him Joseph, praying the LORD would add her another son.
After Joseph was born, Jacob asked Laban to let him return to his own country.
He asked for his wives and children, for whom he had served, so he could leave.
Laban asked him to stay, since he had learned by divination that the LORD had blessed him because of Jacob.
Laban told Jacob to name his wages and he would pay them.
Jacob said Laban knew how he had served him and how his livestock had prospered.
What little Laban had before had grown greatly, and the LORD had blessed him wherever Jacob went; now Jacob wanted to provide for his own household.
Laban asked what he should give him; Jacob said give nothing, but let him keep tending the flocks under one condition.
Let Jacob go through the flocks removing every speckled and spotted sheep and every dark lamb and spotted goat; those would be his wages.
His honesty would testify for him later, since any solid-colored goat or non-speckled sheep found with him would count as stolen.
Laban agreed to the terms.
But that day Laban removed the streaked and spotted animals himself and gave them to his sons.
He put three days' distance between himself and Jacob, who kept tending the rest of Laban's flock.
Jacob took fresh branches of poplar, almond, and plane and peeled white stripes into them.
He set the peeled branches in the watering troughs where the flocks came to drink and mate.
The flocks bred in front of the branches and produced streaked, speckled, and spotted young.
Jacob separated these out and set them apart from Laban's flock, breeding his own herds separately.
Whenever the stronger animals were breeding, Jacob put the branches in the troughs so they would breed in sight of them.
But when the flocks were weak, he did not put the branches out, so the weaker ones went to Laban and the stronger to Jacob.
So Jacob grew exceedingly rich, with large flocks, servants, camels, and donkeys.
